title: Untitled
research_problem: Pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks is not adequately modeled in existing microscopic simulation models. The study addresses the gap in modeling specific behaviors including group and individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and responses to signal control and crosswalk boundary at signalized intersections.
method_summary: A modified social force model was developed for pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized crosswalks. The model incorporates five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. Parameters were calibrated by separating into measurable parameters (directly estimated from observed trajectory data) and non-measurable parameters (derived by maximum likelihood estimation).
tasks: pedestrian_trajectory_estimation; collision_avoidance_modeling; lane_formation_simulation; stop_go_decision_modeling
datasets: North crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City, Japan; 1352 pedestrian trajectories; 124 left-turning vehicle trajectories; 97,200 position samples
metrics: MAPE_position_estimation_5.7%; MAPE_speed_estimation_10.5%; two_sample_ttest_passing_position_distribution; lane_formation_count_comparison
claim_text: The modified social force model accurately reproduces pedestrian trajectory with 5.7% MAPE for position and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation
limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world | Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship | The visual range parameters (170 degrees, 8m) were set by referring to related studies rather than being directly measured from the observed dataset
notes: The study is part of efforts to develop microscopic simulation for safety assessment at signalized intersections. In Japan, more than 30% of traffic accident fatalities are pedestrians at crosswalks. The model addresses gap in existing literature where social force models were not fully developed or calibrated based on real trajectory data for signalized crosswalks. All non-measurable parameters showed statistical significance at 95% confidence level with p-values < 0.05.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a pedestrian micro-simulation model for Chinese traffic conditions that accurately represents the high rate of red light running and mixed traffic characteristics, addressing the limitation that foreign-developed simulation tools cannot effectively describe Chinese pedestrian behavior at signalized intersections
method_summary: A pedestrian behavior model for traffic micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II) types. The model uses questionnaire data to determine pedestrian type proportions and external factor impacts, and videotape analysis to extract time gap (DT) distributions for gap acceptance. Parameters r1 (Type I proportion), r2 (violation followers), and r3 (gap acceptance ratio) are calibrated and adjusted to match field observations.
tasks: Pedestrian road crossing behavior simulation; Red light running decision modeling; Gap acceptance modeling for pedestrian-vehicle interactions
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University with 989 samples
metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Distribution of pedestrian arriving intervals
claim_text: The two-type pedestrian classification (law-obeying vs opportunistic) combined with dynamic parameter adjustment can effectively simulate Chinese pedestrian signal non-compliance behavior
limitations: Model only considers pedestrian decision-making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicles changing speed | Calculation method of DT (safety gap criterion) needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Simplifying assumption of treating turning vehicles as straight-going vehicles reduces realism of simulation | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrian populations and answers may not reflect actual behavior
notes: Model uses DT = T1 - T2 where T1 is time for oncoming vehicle to reach pedestrian and T2 is time for pedestrian to reach safe position; Pedestrian Type I will stop and wait at red light; Type II looks for gaps; Type I can become followers (r2 proportion); Three key parameters: r1 (Type I proportion, calibrated to 0.35-0.55), r2 (follow probability, adjusted to 0.35), r3 (gap acceptance ratio from videotape); External factors considered: policeman presence, other pedestrians behavior, vehicle flow; Study conducted in Xi'an, China with data collection from November 2003 to April 2004

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identifying potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, control) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message, control). Data collected from simulator (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and surveys (receptivity, demographics, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
tasks: walking_across_virtual_crosswalk; rating_external_features; completing_surveys
metrics: feature_ratings_7point_scale; crossing_time; waiting_time; pedestrian_receptivity_score; pedestrian_behavior_questionnaire; personal_innovativeness_score
claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and stopping at intersection - no other traffic included | Features indicated safe crossing conditions only - vehicle stopped in every trial | First exposure to FAV and features - results may differ with familiarity over time | Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability | Non-signalized intersection only - does not account for signalized crossings
notes: VR platform validated by Deb et al. 2017. Used Unity 3D and HTC Vive headset. SAE Level 5 automation (fully autonomous). Features selected based on prior research outcomes and feasibility. Walking speed in simulator was 1.54 m/sec faster than general walking speed 1.20 m/sec.

metrics: feature_ratings_7point_scale; crossing_time; waiting_time; pedestrian_receptivity_score; pedestrian_behavior_questionnaire; personal_innovativeness_score
claim_text: Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly even with external features, while those who violate rules intentionally or get distracted became more cautious
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and stopping at intersection - no other traffic included | Features indicated safe crossing conditions only - vehicle stopped in every trial | First exposure to FAV and features - results may differ with familiarity over time | Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability | Non-signalized intersection only - does not account for signalized crossings

title: Untitled
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the theory of planned behavior (TPB), and extend the theory by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.
method_summary: A questionnaire was designed with a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. Survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 complete and valid responses. Data reliability and validity were evaluated using Cronbach's alpha and confirmatory factor analysis. Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to analyze the relationships between constructs, with maximum likelihood estimation and model fit assessed by CMIN/df, RMSEA, PCLOSE, and CFI indices.
method_family: Structural equation modeling (SEM), Survey-based quantitative research
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions; Test extended TPB model components including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency; Compare predictive utility of basic TPB model versus extended models
datasets: 260 respondents from Dalian, China; demographic variables including age (18-24: 49.2%, 25-39: 33.1%), gender (57.3% female), education (58.5% undergraduate), income (45.9% earned 2000-5000 yuan monthly), driver license status (31.9% held), and driving frequency
metrics: Cronbach's alpha (reliability); Confirmatory factor analysis (validity); CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOSE; CFI (Comparative Fit Index)
claim_text: Instrumental attitude and subjective norm were significant predictors of pedestrian violating behavior intention in the basic TPB model
limitations: Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior - respondents may not accurately report their violating behavior | Relatively small number of items in the questionnaire to maintain internal reliability, which could affect the results | Perceived behavioral control was not significant in this study - may require scenarios with different levels of difficulty to properly evaluate its effect | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may fail to realize or adequately perceive the danger of violating behavior
notes: The study was conducted in Dalian, China and published in Accident Analysis and Prevention in 2016. The paper extends TPB by incorporating Chinese-specific factors like conformity tendency. Model 4 was identified as the best-fitting model with CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030. Key practical implications include designing safety interventions that focus on changing attitudes, correcting social norms through education, and increasing safety awareness.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection in an outdoor environment
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment conducted among 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior (initiate time, look left/right frequency, crossing speed), and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker recorded visual attention allocation (pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation points, fixation time, fixation duration). Four conditions were compared: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Repeated-measure ANOVA with Bonferroni-corrected post-hoc comparisons was used for analysis.
tasks: Pedestrian crossing at signalized intersection under different mobile phone distraction conditions
metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation); Look left and right (frequency while crossing); Crossing speed (mean walking speed); Pupil diameter (mean left eye diameter); Scanning frequency (scans per unit time); Fixation points (count on five areas of interest); Fixation time (total duration on five areas of interest); Fixation duration (mean duration on five areas of interest)
claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
limitations: Small sample size (n=28) and limitation to college students limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population | Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute) | Participants used unfamiliar experimental phone and mental arithmetic tasks rather than naturalistic phone use | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation during experiment | Only three types of mobile phone distraction were studied; other tasks like mobile internet browsing not included
notes: First outdoor-environment study comparing three types of mobile phone distraction (music, conversation, text) on pedestrian crossing; Used Tobii Pro Glasses 2 wearable eye-tracker for objective visual attention measurement; Five areas of interest (AOIs) analyzed: mobile phone area, crosswalk area, traffic signal area, right traffic area, left traffic area; Study addresses gap in literature where virtual-environment experiments may lack perceived risk similar to outdoor environments; Results support need for pedestrian safety policy interventions and design considerations

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify the personal and environmental factors that affect the propensity of red light running violations by pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, including the effects of social influences and interaction effects between factors.
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong to collect data on pedestrian crossing behavior, demographics, traffic conditions, and signal timing. A random parameter logit model was developed to analyze the association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors.
method_family: Discrete choice modeling, random parameter logit (mixed logit)
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior; Interaction effect analysis between personal and environmental factors
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; Crosswalks located at Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
metrics: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for model comparison; Odds ratios for factor effects; Z-values for statistical significance
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667), and this effect varies with traffic volume and presence of other violators
limitations: The study focuses on propensity rather than safety outcomes (crash risk) - the association between red light running and actual crash involvement is not measured | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | The random parameter approach accounts for unobserved heterogeneity in risk-taking attitude and safety perception, but these individual-level psychological factors are not directly measured | The study is limited to urban Hong Kong crosswalks without pedestrian countdown displays; findings may not generalize to other contexts with different signal systems
notes: Study addresses a significant safety issue - 62% of road fatalities in Hong Kong are pedestrians, and 25% of pedestrian crashes at signalized intersections involve red light violations; The random parameter approach is justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect, indicating unobserved heterogeneity among female pedestrians; Hong Kong uses three-phase pedestrian signals (steady green, flashing green, steady red) without countdown displays; Key interaction effects discovered include: gender x traffic volume, gender x others violated, elderly x with companion, others violated x with companion; Model 2 with interaction terms showed superior performance with lower AIC (2750.1 vs 2776.6)

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections and developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic light signals
method_summary: The paper proposes a bounded Pareto distribution-based model for pedestrian waiting times, uses generalized linear modeling to relate waiting time to vehicle time headway (excess headway), applies statistical unconditioning to integrate over vehicle headway distribution, and employs EM algorithm for parameter estimation. The final model is a mixture of four component distributions representing different pedestrian types and traffic conditions.
method_family: Statistical modeling, mixture models, bounded distributions, survival analysis alternative
tasks: Pedestrian intended waiting time modeling; Model parameter estimation; Goodness-of-fit testing; Model selection using BIC; Subgroup analysis by age and gender
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China: 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days from 10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.; red-man phase duration = 75 seconds; total traffic-light cycle = 110 seconds
metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test; BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion) for model selection; Parameter standard errors; Proportions of pedestrian categories (r1, r2, r4); Median intended waiting time; Average intended waiting time
claim_text: The U-shaped distribution characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior during the red-man phase, showing that there are large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those willing to wait for the entire phase
limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only waiting time as the explicit variable; multivariate approaches considering multiple risk factors simultaneously are not developed | Parameter identifiability can be an issue for mixture distributions with discrete components | Data collection was limited to weekend days (Saturday and Sunday) in Kunming, China; generalizability to other locations, weekdays, or cultural contexts is uncertain | Age groups were based on observer best-effort guesses rather than verified ages, introducing potential classification error | The model assumes the vehicle time headway follows Cowan's model; departures from this assumption may affect model accuracy
notes: This paper addresses a significant gap in pedestrian behavior research - methodological development of pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections. The proposed model accounts for the bounded nature of waiting times (limited by red-man phase duration) and captures the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians through vehicle time headway. The empirical study found that approximately 13.8% of pedestrians cross immediately, 50.6% wait for entire red-man phase, and 35.6% wait then cross when finding an acceptable gap. Model was validated using chi-square goodness-of-fit test at 5% significance level.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections and developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic light signals
method_summary: The paper proposes a bounded Pareto distribution-based model for pedestrian waiting times, uses generalized linear modeling to relate waiting time to vehicle time headway (excess headway), applies statistical unconditioning to integrate over vehicle headway distribution, and employs EM algorithm for parameter estimation. The final model is a mixture of four component distributions representing different pedestrian types and traffic conditions.
method_family: Statistical modeling, mixture models, bounded distributions, survival analysis alternative
tasks: Pedestrian intended waiting time modeling; Model parameter estimation; Goodness-of-fit testing; Model selection using BIC; Subgroup analysis by age and gender
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China: 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days from 10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.; red-man phase duration = 75 seconds; total traffic-light cycle = 110 seconds
metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test; BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion) for model selection; Parameter standard errors; Proportions of pedestrian categories (r1, r2, r4); Median intended waiting time; Average intended waiting time
claim_text: The proposed bounded waiting time model is more suitable than exponential distribution for pedestrian crossings at signalized intersections because exponential cannot capture the bounded nature and tail behavior near the red-man phase upper bound
limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only waiting time as the explicit variable; multivariate approaches considering multiple risk factors simultaneously are not developed | Parameter identifiability can be an issue for mixture distributions with discrete components | Data collection was limited to weekend days (Saturday and Sunday) in Kunming, China; generalizability to other locations, weekdays, or cultural contexts is uncertain | Age groups were based on observer best-effort guesses rather than verified ages, introducing potential classification error | The model assumes the vehicle time headway follows Cowan's model; departures from this assumption may affect model accuracy
notes: This paper addresses a significant gap in pedestrian behavior research - methodological development of pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections. The proposed model accounts for the bounded nature of waiting times (limited by red-man phase duration) and captures the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians through vehicle time headway. The empirical study found that approximately 13.8% of pedestrians cross immediately, 50.6% wait for entire red-man phase, and 35.6% wait then cross when finding an acceptable gap. Model was validated using chi-square goodness-of-fit test at 5% significance level.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections in China, which is a critical factor causing pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The study aims to explore factors affecting this behavior to propose countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related crashes.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrian socio-economic characteristics, trip features, and attitude variables. Using 631 valid samples from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrian red-light running behavior.
tasks: analyzing_factors_affecting_pedestrian_red_light_running; predicting_probability_of_red_light_running_behavior
datasets: Survey data from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); 631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections
metrics: score_test; odds_ratio; p_value_significance
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and behavioral differences | Study only considered signalized intersections, not other crossing facilities like midblock crossings | Focused on individual pedestrian behavior, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group effects
notes: The study fills a gap in the literature by focusing on Chinese pedestrians and considering attitude-related factors alongside individual characteristics and trip features. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function for red-light running behavior.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify factors that affect the propensity of red light running of pedestrians at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (pedestrians' demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red (pedestrian) signal at six signalized crosswalks in both peak and non-peak periods were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure the association between propensity of red light running violation and possible explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian crossing decisions.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (discrete choice model)
tasks: Analyze factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity; Examine interaction effects between personal and environmental factors; Measure association between red light running violation and explanatory variables including demographics, signal time, traffic condition, and social influences
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed during 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong - sites were hot spots of pedestrian crashes with moderate pedestrian and vehicular traffic volumes
metrics: Binary red light running violation (yes/no); Parameter coefficients; Odds ratios; Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for model comparison; Marginal effects (elasticities)
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b = -0.433, odds ratio = 0.667)
limitations: Safety outcome (associated crash risk) is out of study scope - only behavioral propensity examined, not actual crash consequences | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior is not assessed despite being correlated with risk and severity | Information on safety perception and attitude of pedestrians not available in observation survey - attitudinal factors not directly measured | Only pedestrian signal phases in Hong Kong studied (green, flashing green, red) - may not generalize to other jurisdictions with different signal systems

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, compliance/noncompliance with traffic signals, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions in highly populous cities like Mumbai, India
method_summary: Video cameras recorded pedestrian behavior at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours. 775 pedestrian samples were analyzed using statistical tests (Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA, Student t test) to identify significant factors affecting crossing speed, compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions. Logistic regression models were developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and interaction.
method_family: Statistical Analysis (ANOVA, Pearson correlation, Student t-test, Logistic Regression)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variations at signalized intersections; Identify factors affecting pedestrian compliance/noncompliance with traffic signals; Analyze pedestrian-vehicular interactions in crosswalks; Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai: Swami Vivekanand Junction (166 samples), Mahim Junction (272 samples), Holkar Junction (337 samples)
metrics: Crossing speed (mean, SD, 15th/50th/85th percentile); Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF); Pedestrian compliance rate (%); Odds Ratio (OR) from logistic regression; F-values and P-values from statistical tests
claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with observed mean speed of 1.34 m/s in Mumbai
limitations: Sample size of 775 pedestrians may not be representative of all pedestrian populations in mixed traffic conditions | Study conducted only in Mumbai, India - findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic patterns and cultural behaviors | Data collected only during peak hours (morning and evening) - may not capture off-peak behavior patterns
notes: First study examining pedestrian crossing speed variation and pedestrian-vehicular interaction in crosswalks with comprehensive parameters in Indian context; Introduces new metric CSDF (Crossing Speed Deviation Factor) for understanding pedestrian speed variations; Only 44% overall pedestrian compliance rate observed, with significant variation across junctions (34%-64%); Adult pedestrians have highest odds (8.074) of interaction compared to children and elderly based on logistic regression

claim_text: Logistic regression models successfully identify significant factors affecting pedestrian noncompliance (OR values) and pedestrian-vehicular interactions
limitations: Sample size of 775 pedestrians may not be representative of all pedestrian populations in mixed traffic conditions | Study conducted only in Mumbai, India - findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic patterns and cultural behaviors | Data collected only during peak hours (morning and evening) - may not capture off-peak behavior patterns
notes: First study examining pedestrian crossing speed variation and pedestrian-vehicular interaction in crosswalks with comprehensive parameters in Indian context; Introduces new metric CSDF (Crossing Speed Deviation Factor) for understanding pedestrian speed variations; Only 44% overall pedestrian compliance rate observed, with significant variation across junctions (34%-64%); Adult pedestrians have highest odds (8.074) of interaction compared to children and elderly based on logistic regression
